title: 32-bit multiplier into accumulator — setup miss at 1.2 GHz
description:
# Multiplier accumulator setup violation

The DSP core has a 32-bit multiplier feeding an accumulator register. At 1.2 GHz (833 ps period), `report_timing` flags -350 ps on `mult_in_reg/Q -> acc_reg/D`.

The architect confirms: software always issues a NOP after a multiply, so the accumulator doesn't need to capture the multiplier result in the same cycle — it has 2 cycles.

The netlist is fine. Fix it in SDC.

**Note:** when you apply a multicycle setup exception, you must also think about hold — but for this problem, pick the setup fix and you'll score.

reportedViolation.summary: setup VIOLATED (-0.350 ns) on mult_in_reg/Q -> acc_reg/D

rootCauseOptions.label: The multiplier datapath is architecturally two cycles — a multicycle_path exception is missing
mult_pp, mult_csa, mult_cpa all need resizing
Wire delays on n_csa_y are too high and need routing cleanup
The path is actually a false path and should be excluded entirely

acceptedFixes.description: Add `set_multicycle_path 2 -setup -from mult_in_reg/Q -to acc_reg/D` plus the matching `-hold 1` to keep hold checks aligned
Insert a pipeline stage inside the multiplier (adds a cycle of latency but relaxes each stage)

hintTree.nudge: The architect gave you extra information about software behavior. That's always a hint about timing exceptions.
hintTree.hint: Software issues a NOP after the multiply — meaning the result has two cycles to stabilize before the accumulator needs it. Which SDC construct captures that?
hintTree.walkthrough: Run `report_exceptions` — empty. The path is ~1.53 ns long; a single cycle is 833 ps. That's ~1.83 cycles. Apply `set_multicycle_path 2 -setup -from mult_in_reg/Q -to acc_reg/D`. Always pair with `set_multicycle_path 1 -hold ...` so hold checks stay aligned. Real netlist fix (pipelining) also works but costs area and changes latency.

steps.title: Read the timing report
steps.goal: Look at the multiplier path delay. How does it compare to the clock period?

checkpoint.question: How many clock cycles does this path actually need?

steps.insight: The multiplier path goes through partial products (420 ps), carry-save tree (380 ps), carry-propagate adder (260 ps), plus CK→Q and wires. Total ~1.53 ns — nearly 2 full cycles at 1.2 GHz.

steps.title: Examine the datapath stages
steps.goal: Look at each multiplier block. Are any of them unreasonably slow, or is this just a naturally long path?

checkpoint.question: Is resizing these blocks going to fix the violation?

options.label: No — each block's delay is inherent to its function. A 32-bit multiply is fundamentally multi-cycle at 1.2 GHz.
Yes — sizing up mult_csa and mult_cpa will close timing

steps.insight: MULT_PP (partial products), CSA_TREE (carry-save), CPA_32 (carry-propagate) — these are all big arithmetic blocks. Their delays are inherent to the operation. You can't size a 32-bit multiplier into a single 833 ps cycle at reasonable PPA.

steps.title: Check the architectural intent
steps.goal: Re-read the problem description. The architect gave you critical information about software behavior.

checkpoint.question: What did the architect tell you?

options.label: Software always issues a NOP after a multiply, so the accumulator has 2 cycles to capture — and no exceptions are defined
The path should be false-pathed entirely

steps.insight: The NOP means acc_reg doesn't sample until cycle 2 after the multiply issues. The path has 2 full cycles — 1.67 ns — to stabilize. But STA is checking it at 1 cycle because nobody wrote the exception.

steps.title: Apply the correct SDC constraint
steps.goal: Choose between multicycle_path and false_path. Think about what's semantically correct.

options.label: set_multicycle_path 2 -setup (with matching -hold 1) — the path is real but has 2 cycles
No SDC change — pipeline the multiplier instead

steps.insight: set_multicycle_path 2 is correct. Unlike a false_path (which removes *all* timing checks), multicycle keeps STA active but shifts the capture edge to cycle 2. Always pair with -hold 1 to keep hold checks aligned. This is the bread-and-butter SDC move for arithmetic datapaths.
